title: Out of work, out of the labour force? Attachment, search effort and participation flows
summary: This Bank of England Staff Working Paper (No. 1,178) examines how compositional changes in the unemployment pool affect labour market dynamics. Using UK Labour Force Survey data, the authors find that workers entering unemployment from job loss are substantially less likely to exit the labour force than those entering from inactivity, making prior employment status the strongest predictor of labour market attachment. The paper extends a Diamond-Mortensen-Pissarides search-and-matching model to incorporate heterogeneous labour market attachment, demonstrating that this mechanism increases unemployment volatility by approximately 50%.
key_points: Workers who enter unemployment following job loss are less likely to exit the labour force than workers who enter from inactivity, making prior employment status the strongest predictor of labour market attachment
The procyclicality in the rate at which unemployed workers leave the labour force is strongly correlated with compositional changes in the unemployment pool
Search effort variation does not explain the differences in labour market attachment between these groups
Extending a Diamond-Mortensen-Pissarides search-and-matching model to allow for heterogeneous labour market attachment amplifies unemployment fluctuations relative to the standard model
The compositional mechanism increases unemployment volatility by around 50%
The model helps account for the sharp rise in unemployment at the onset of the Great Recession
Authors: Tomas Key, Matthew McKernan, and Bradley Speigner
why_it_matters: This research provides a structural explanation for why unemployment can spike sharply during recessions even without proportional increases in job losses. By showing that the composition of the unemployment pool—specifically whether workers came from employment versus inactivity—affects their probability of leaving the labour force, the paper offers a more nuanced understanding of labour market dynamics. This could inform BoE forecasts and policy assessments of labour market slack, particularly during economic downturns when the composition of unemployment shifts.
macro_context: This research contributes to understanding labour market dynamics and the transmission of economic shocks. The finding that compositional changes amplify unemployment volatility by 50% has implications for interpreting labour market data and assessing economic slack. The mechanism helps explain the sharp rise in unemployment during the Great Recession, suggesting that during severe downturns, the influx of workers from employment into unemployment (who have high labour force attachment) temporarily inflates unemployment figures before they subsequently exit the labour force. This has implications for central bank assessments of labour market conditions and the natural rate of unemployment.
important_numbers: 50% (increase in unemployment volatility explained by the compositional mechanism)
